title: Organizational (corporate) culture
summary: Organizational culture is 'the way things are done around here' — the shared values, beliefs and norms that shape how employees actually behave once the org chart is put away. This HL-only lesson works through the whole of syllabus 2.5: what organizational culture means and the elements that form and express it; Handy's four types (power, role, task and person culture); how culture influences employee behaviour and business performance; how and why culture changes and why changing it is so difficult; and culture clashes after a merger, acquisition or in multinational firms. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see why a supported judgement, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Define organizational (corporate) culture as 'the way things are done around here' and identify the elements that form and express it.
Distinguish Handy's four types of culture — power, role, task and person — and classify a business using evidence.
Explain how organizational culture influences employee behaviour and business performance.
Explain how and why organizational culture changes, and analyse why culture is so difficult to change.
Evaluate the causes and consequences of culture clashes after a merger or acquisition and in multinational firms, reaching a supported judgement.

simpleExplanation.title: The personality of a business
simpleExplanation.summary: Organizational culture is the personality of a company — the unwritten rules, shared values and habits that decide how people actually behave once the formal rules are put away. It is often summed up as 'the way things are done around here', and it quietly shapes everything from motivation to whether a merger succeeds or falls apart.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a friendship group. It has inside jokes, an unspoken sense of how you talk to each other, and things you all quietly value. A newcomer has to learn that 'vibe' to fit in — nobody hands them a rulebook. A company's culture works the same way but at scale: it tells employees how to make decisions, how to treat customers, and what really gets rewarded, none of which appears on the organization chart. And just like a friendship group, when two very different groups are forced together — say after a merger — the clash of habits can be painful and slow to settle.
steps: Read the culture from its signs — office layout, dress code, how meetings run, what stories get told and who gets promoted reveal the values underneath.
Classify it using Handy's four types: power (central boss), role (rules and hierarchy), task (project teams) or person (the experts come first).
Trace how that culture drives employee behaviour and performance — motivation, innovation, loyalty and customer service all flow from it.
Judge what happens when culture must change or two cultures collide — expect resistance, cost and time, and weigh whether the effort is worth it for this business.

flashcards.back: The shared values, attitudes, beliefs and norms that are accepted and enacted within an organization — often summed up as 'the way things are done around here'. It is the informal personality of a business, distinct from its formal structure.

The unwritten rules of expected behaviour within an organization — how people speak in meetings, whether emails are formal, whether staying late is expected. Norms are how culture shows up in day-to-day conduct.

The visible, tangible signs of culture — office design, dress code, logos, rituals and stories. They are easy to observe but can be hard to interpret, because the same artefact can mean different things.

Control radiates from a central figure or small group, like a spider's web. Decisions are fast because few people make them, but the culture can be autocratic and stressful and depends heavily on that central person. Common in small or founder-led firms.

Culture built on clearly defined roles inside a formal, bureaucratic hierarchy. Rules, procedures and the job description matter more than the individual. It is stable and predictable but slow to adapt and can stifle creativity. Common in large, established organizations.

Power and focus sit with project teams assembled to get a specific job done; authority shifts to whoever has the relevant expertise. Flexible, collaborative and motivating, but can lack central control and lead to conflicting priorities. Common in consultancies and tech firms.

The individual expert is the focal point; the organization exists to support its members rather than the other way round. Common in professional partnerships of lawyers, doctors or architects. Motivating for professionals but hard to coordinate around a shared strategy.

Culture is shaped by the founder's values and vision, the history and stories of the firm, its national and industry context, its leadership and reward systems, and who it recruits and promotes. Over time these harden into shared assumptions that new staff absorb.

A distinct culture within one part of an organization — for example, a creative marketing team inside an otherwise bureaucratic firm. Large businesses are often a blend of cultures rather than a single one.

Conflict between two or more incompatible organizational cultures — over communication style, pace of decision-making, values or attitudes to authority. A leading cause of failure in mergers and acquisitions, and a common problem in multinational firms.

flashcards.front: Why culture is hard to change
flashcards.back: Culture lives in deeply held, often unconscious assumptions and habits, not just in written rules. Employees resist losing what is familiar, existing power structures defend the status quo, and genuine change takes years of consistent leadership, recruitment and reward — you cannot change it by rewriting a mission statement.

Structure is the formal skeleton — roles, reporting lines and the organization chart. Culture is the informal personality — how people actually behave within that skeleton. Structure is official and rigid; culture is social and fluid. Changing the chart does not automatically change the culture.

Announcing new values, a rebrand or a fresh mission statement does NOT change a culture. Real change means shifting ingrained habits and assumptions, which is slow, costly and often resisted — expect years, not weeks.

In a POWER culture authority sits with a central person regardless of expertise; in a TASK culture authority shifts to whoever has the relevant expertise for the project. Fast decisions alone do not make a culture a power culture — check WHERE the power comes from.

quickCheck.prompt: A firm assembles cross-functional project teams for each product launch, and leadership on a project shifts to whoever has the most relevant expertise rather than to the most senior person. Using Handy's model, this is best described as a:
options: Power culture, because decisions are made quickly
Role culture, because there are defined teams
Task culture, because power shifts to expertise to complete specific projects
Person culture, because individuals are involved
quickCheck.answer: Task culture, because power shifts to expertise to complete specific projects

quickCheck.prompt: In a large traditional bank, staff follow detailed procedures, the job description matters more than the individual, and authority flows through a clear hierarchy. Which culture does Handy's model identify, and what is its main weakness?
options: Power culture; it depends too heavily on one central figure
Role culture; it is stable but slow to adapt and can stifle creativity
Task culture; it lacks central control
Person culture; individuals pursue conflicting goals
quickCheck.answer: Role culture; it is stable but slow to adapt and can stifle creativity

quickCheck.prompt: Why is deep organizational culture generally difficult to change?
options: Because it is written down in the mission statement and hard to reprint
Because it lives in ingrained habits and assumptions and is defended by existing power structures, so change is slow and resisted
Because employees are legally required to keep the existing culture
Because culture is fixed by the organization chart and cannot be altered
quickCheck.answer: Because it lives in ingrained habits and assumptions and is defended by existing power structures, so change is slow and resisted

quickCheck.prompt: A hierarchical, procedure-driven manufacturer acquires a fast-moving tech start-up and imposes its own rules and approval layers. Its best engineers quickly leave. This outcome is best explained as a:
options: Culture clash between a role culture and a task culture after the acquisition
Failure of the organization chart to include the new staff
Change in the legal structure of the business
Successful integration because rules were standardised
quickCheck.answer: Culture clash between a role culture and a task culture after the acquisition

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement correctly distinguishes organizational culture from organizational structure?
options: Structure is the informal personality of the firm; culture is the formal chart
Culture and structure are two names for the same thing
Structure is the formal framework of roles and reporting lines; culture is how people actually behave within it, so changing the chart does not automatically change the culture
Culture can be changed instantly by redrawing the structure
quickCheck.answer: Structure is the formal framework of roles and reporting lines; culture is how people actually behave within it, so changing the chart does not automatically change the culture

faq.a: It is the set of shared values, beliefs, norms and assumptions that govern how people behave inside an organization — famously summed up as 'the way things are done around here'. It is the informal personality of the business: not what the rulebook says, but what people actually do, value and reward in practice. You read it from signs like how meetings run, what stories get retold, how people dress, and above all who gets promoted.

faq.q: How is organizational culture different from organizational structure?
faq.a: Structure is the formal skeleton of the business — its roles, responsibilities and reporting lines, all of which appear on an organization chart. Culture is the informal spirit or personality — how people actually behave within that skeleton. Structure is official and rigid; culture is social and fluid. Crucially, redrawing the chart does not automatically change the culture, which is why restructures so often fail to deliver the behaviour change leaders hoped for.

faq.q: How and why does culture change?
faq.a: Culture shifts when the forces that formed it shift — a new leader with different values, a crisis that discredits the old way, a merger, rapid growth, new technology, or a changed market that punishes old habits. Businesses deliberately try to change culture to improve performance: to become more innovative, more customer-focused, safer or more ethical. But intent is not enough — genuine change needs consistent leadership, new role models, and recruitment and reward systems aligned to the new values over a sustained period.

faq.q: Why is culture so difficult to change?
faq.a: Because culture lives in deeply held, often unconscious assumptions and habits rather than in written rules. People resist losing what is familiar and fear what a new culture means for their status; existing power structures actively defend the old way; and long-serving staff embody the old norms. So change is slow (often years), costly (training, communication, sometimes turnover) and uncertain. Rewriting a mission statement or launching a rebrand changes the words, not the behaviour — which is why so many culture-change programmes stall.

faq.q: Why do culture clashes happen after a merger or in a multinational firm?
faq.a: A merger or acquisition forces together two organizations that each formed their own habits, values and pace of decision-making. When a rule-bound role culture combines with a fast-moving task culture, differences in communication, authority and speed create friction, demotivate staff and can trigger a loss of key talent — destroying the very value the deal was meant to capture. Multinationals face a related problem: national cultures differ in attitudes to hierarchy, teamwork and communication, so a management style that motivates staff in one country can demotivate them in another.

sections.content: A business's financial statements tell you what it earns; its culture tells you how it behaves when no one is checking. Organizational (corporate) culture is the shared set of values, beliefs and norms that shape how people actually work together — 'the way things are done around here'. It never appears on the organization chart, yet it quietly decides how decisions get made, how customers are treated, how innovative the firm can be, and whether a merger succeeds or tears itself apart. This HL-only lesson covers the whole of syllabus 2.5 — what culture means and the elements that form and express it, Handy's four types, how culture drives employee behaviour and performance, how and why it changes, why changing it is so hard, and why cultures clash after mergers and in multinational firms. Because 2.5 is an evaluation-heavy topic, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why an applied, supported judgement outscores a memorised list every time.

Organizational culture is the collection of shared values, beliefs, norms and assumptions that govern how people behave within an organization. It is the firm's informal personality — the unwritten 'way things are done around here'. Some cultures are formal and cautious; others are energetic and risk-taking. You cannot see culture directly, so you read it from its expressions: the office layout, the dress code, how meetings are run, the stories staff tell about the firm's past, the way people speak to authority, and — most tellingly — who gets rewarded and promoted, because that reveals what the organization truly values rather than what it merely says it values.

items: **Values:** the core principles the firm treats as important — for example 'customer first', 'safety above all', 'move fast'.
**Beliefs and assumptions:** the convictions, often unconscious, that people take for granted about how the business and its world work.
**Norms:** the unwritten rules of expected behaviour — how to act in meetings, how formally to communicate, whether it is normal to stay late.
**Artefacts:** the visible signs of culture — office design, dress code, logos, rituals, ceremonies and stories — easy to observe but sometimes hard to interpret.
**How culture is formed:** the founder's vision, the firm's history and heroes, its national and industry context, its leadership and reward systems, and who it recruits and promotes.

sections.content: Charles Handy's model classifies organizational cultures into four types, which gives us a shared vocabulary for analysing a business and predicting how it behaves. Two cautions before applying it. First, many organizations are a blend, or contain subcultures — a creative marketing team can run a task culture inside an otherwise bureaucratic role culture. Second, the type is judged by WHERE power and focus sit, not by surface impressions: fast decisions do not automatically mean a power culture, because a task culture can also move fast. Look for the source of authority.

items: **Power culture:** control radiates from a central figure or small group, like a spider's web. Decisions are fast because few people make them. Strengths: speed and clear direction. Weaknesses: can be autocratic and stressful, and depends dangerously on one person. Common in small or founder-led firms.

**Role culture:** built on defined roles inside a formal, bureaucratic hierarchy; rules and the job description matter more than the individual. Strengths: stability, consistency, predictability. Weaknesses: slow to adapt and stifles creativity. Common in large, established organizations and public bodies.

**Task culture:** power sits with project teams assembled to complete a specific job, and authority shifts to whoever has the relevant expertise. Strengths: flexible, collaborative, motivating. Weaknesses: weaker central control and possible conflicting priorities. Common in consultancies, agencies and tech firms.

**Person culture:** the individual expert is the focal point and the organization exists to support its members. Strengths: highly motivating for skilled professionals. Weaknesses: hard to coordinate around a shared strategy. Common in partnerships of lawyers, doctors or architects.

sections.question: An established law firm, 'Marlowe & Reed LLP', operates as a partnership. Each senior lawyer manages their own clients and cases with significant autonomy, and the central administration exists mainly to provide accounting and IT support. Using Handy's model, analyse the organizational culture at Marlowe & Reed and explain one advantage and one disadvantage of this culture. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Marlowe & Reed most closely exhibits a **person culture** in Handy's model. The evidence is that the firm is organised to serve the individuals within it — the senior lawyers — who work with significant autonomy over their own clients and cases, while the central administration acts as a support structure rather than a controlling hierarchy. Authority rests on the professional expertise and status of the individual lawyers, not on a rigid chain of command or a single dominant figure.

One advantage of this person culture is strong professional motivation. As experts trusted to run their own caseloads, the lawyers enjoy high autonomy, which meets higher-level psychological needs and tends to produce high-quality work and low turnover among valued partners.

One disadvantage is the difficulty of steering the firm as a whole. Because each lawyer prioritises their own clients, it can be very hard to agree a unified long-term strategy, which may leave the firm slow to react to market change and prone to internal disputes over shared resources.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks** — the command term 'analyse' expects AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to Marlowe & Reed, and AO3 developed reasoning:

- **AO1 (knowledge):** correctly identifies the person culture and shows understanding of what defines it (the organization serves the individual experts).
- **AO2 (application):** ties the classification to the firm's specifics — lawyers' autonomy over their own clients, admin as a support function.
- **AO3 (analysis):** develops one advantage (autonomy → motivation → quality/retention) and one disadvantage (individual priorities → weak shared strategy) as chains of reasoning, not bare labels.

The engine credits developed, applied points: naming 'person culture' with no evidence from the firm, or listing an advantage without explaining its effect, stays in the lower bands.

sections.content: In Paper 1 you must justify your culture classification with evidence from the case study, not assert it. Do not just write 'the business has a task culture'. Write 'the business likely has a task culture, as shown by its cross-functional project teams and its focus on launch deadlines'. The classification earns AO1; the evidence link earns the AO2 application marks that lift the answer.
How culture influences behaviour and performance

Culture matters because it shapes what employees actually do. A culture sets expectations that guide behaviour far more powerfully than any rulebook: it influences how hard people work, how much they innovate, how they treat customers, how safely they operate and how loyal they feel. A strong, positive culture aligned to the firm's goals can lift motivation, cohesion and performance — a well-matched task culture, for instance, meets a creative employee's needs for autonomy and achievement and so acts as a motivator. A misaligned or toxic culture does the reverse: it becomes a source of dissatisfaction, raises staff turnover and absenteeism, damages the employer brand, and can even normalise unethical or unsafe conduct. Culture is therefore a genuine driver of business performance, not a soft extra — but its effect depends on the fit between the culture and the strategy the firm is trying to pursue.

items: **Motivation and retention:** a culture that fits employees' needs raises motivation and loyalty; a misaligned one drives dissatisfaction and turnover.
**Innovation and speed:** task and power cultures can move and adapt quickly; a rigid role culture can slow change and smother new ideas.
**Customer experience and reputation:** norms about how customers are treated flow straight to service quality and brand image.
**Ethics and risk:** culture sets what behaviour is quietly tolerated, so it shapes safety, compliance and ethical conduct.
**Fit matters:** there is no single 'best' culture — performance depends on how well the culture matches the firm's strategy and market.

sections.content: How and why culture changes — and why it is so hard

Culture is not fixed. It changes when the forces that formed it change: a new leader with different values arrives, a crisis discredits the old way of working, the firm grows rapidly or goes global, new technology reshapes work, or a merger throws two cultures together. Businesses also try to change culture deliberately, to become more innovative, more customer-focused, safer or more ethical. But deliberate culture change is one of the hardest things a business can attempt. Culture lives in deeply held, often unconscious assumptions and habits, not just in written rules — so it cannot be switched by rewriting a mission statement or launching a rebrand. Employees resist losing what is familiar and fear what change means for their status; existing power structures defend the old way; and long-serving staff embody the old norms. Genuine change therefore demands consistent leadership modelling the new behaviour, aligned recruitment and reward systems, and sustained effort over years — with real costs in training, communication and sometimes staff turnover, and no guarantee of success.

items: **Triggers of change:** new leadership, a crisis or scandal, rapid growth or globalisation, new technology, or a merger/acquisition.
**Why firms change culture:** to lift innovation, improve customer focus, strengthen safety or ethics, or align a merged business.
**Why it is hard — resistance:** people fear losing the familiar and their status; existing power holders defend the status quo.
**Why it is hard — depth and time:** culture sits in unconscious assumptions and habits, so change is slow (often years), not instant.
**Why it is hard — cost and uncertainty:** training, communication and possible turnover cost money, and success is never guaranteed.
**What genuine change needs:** leaders who model the new behaviour, plus recruitment and reward systems aligned to the new values.

sections.question: Discuss the challenges a business may face when trying to change its organizational culture. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer** (applied to a worked context — a large, hierarchical insurance company, 'Harbourline', whose new CEO wants to shift its slow, rule-bound role culture toward a faster, more customer-focused and innovative culture).

The first and deepest challenge is that Harbourline's culture lives in ingrained habits and unconscious assumptions, not merely in its written procedures. After decades as a role culture, staff instinctively 'follow the process' and defer to the hierarchy. The CEO can rewrite the mission statement and announce new values overnight, but that changes only the words on the wall — not how a claims handler actually behaves on a Monday morning. Because the target is behaviour rather than rules, change is inherently slow and hard to reach.

A second major challenge is resistance from employees. People fear losing what is familiar and worry what a new, faster culture means for their status and job security. Long-serving managers who built their careers under the role culture may quietly protect the old way, because the existing power structure benefits them. This resistance can show up as low morale, informal non-cooperation or higher staff turnover, all of which slow the change and can damage performance in the meantime.

A third challenge is the time and cost involved. Genuine culture change at Harbourline would require sustained, consistent leadership modelling the new behaviour, retraining thousands of staff, and re-engineering recruitment and reward systems so that innovation and customer focus — not rule-following — get promoted. This takes years and significant money, and the disruption can hurt short-term results. There is also no guarantee of success: many culture-change programmes stall once the initial push fades and old habits reassert themselves.

On the other hand, the challenges are not insurmountable, and the potential benefits are large. If Harbourline can shift toward a task-oriented, customer-focused culture, it could respond faster to competitors, innovate its products and lift customer satisfaction — a real competitive advantage in a slow-moving industry. The change is more achievable if the CEO leads by example, communicates a clear and compelling vision, involves employees so they own the change rather than have it imposed, and aligns rewards to the new behaviour. A crisis or a burning platform (for example, losing customers to a nimble insurtech rival) can also weaken resistance by making the old way visibly unsustainable.

**Judgement.** On balance, changing Harbourline's culture is possible but genuinely difficult, and success depends less on the CEO's intentions than on execution over time. The decisive challenge is depth combined with resistance: because the old role culture is embedded in habits and defended by the managers who benefit from it, a quick top-down relaunch will fail, and the firm must accept years of consistent, costly effort with reward and recruitment systems doing the heavy lifting. The change is worth attempting given the competitive threat, but Harbourline should expect the hardest obstacle to be its own people's understandable resistance, and should manage the change gradually and participatively rather than by decree.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks** — 'discuss' is a top-level command term needing AO1, AO2 and a balanced AO3 evaluation ending in a supported judgement:

- **AO1 — knowledge (culture concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the ideas the question turns on — organizational culture as shared values and assumptions, why deep culture resists change, the role of existing power structures, and what genuine change requires (leadership, recruitment, reward). Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to the business:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to the specific firm — Harbourline's entrenched role culture, the claims handlers' 'follow the process' habit, long-serving managers defending the old way, the insurtech competitive threat. Generic points that never touch the business do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — balanced analysis + supported judgement:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning — the challenges (depth of habit, employee resistance, time and cost, risk of failure) weighed against the mitigants and benefits (clear vision, leading by example, participation, aligned rewards, a crisis that weakens resistance) — and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to a conclusion and justifies WHY the decisive challenge is resistance plus depth for this firm.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus judgement. The engine rewards an answer that applies culture theory to the business and ends in a reasoned conclusion; it does NOT reward a longer generic list of 'why culture is hard to change' with no business and no decision.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-challenges answers with no balance; a 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without choosing; and correct definitions of culture with no link to the firm.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right culture concepts, AO2 for applying them to the business in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So do not just list reasons culture is hard to change: name the concept (ingrained assumptions, resistance, aligned reward systems), apply it to the specific business, weigh the challenges against how they could be overcome, and finish by committing to a judgement with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.
Culture clashes: mergers, acquisitions and multinationals

A culture clash is conflict between two or more incompatible organizational cultures. It is one of the most common reasons mergers and acquisitions fail. When a firm with a rigid, hierarchical role culture combines with a flexible, innovative task culture, differences in communication style, decision-making speed, values and attitudes to authority create friction, demotivate staff and can trigger a loss of key talent — destroying the very value the deal was meant to capture. Multinational firms face a related version of the problem: national cultures differ in their attitudes to hierarchy, teamwork, risk and directness, so a management approach that motivates staff in one country can confuse or demotivate them in another. In both cases the lesson is the same — the technical or financial logic of a deal or an expansion can be sound, yet the human reality of two cultures colliding is what determines whether it works.

sections.question: 'AutoStark', a traditional car manufacturer with a strong role culture, acquires 'VoltDrive', a dynamic electric-vehicle start-up with a task culture. Evaluate the likely consequences of this acquisition for the employees of VoltDrive. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The acquisition brings together two very different cultures — AutoStark's rule-bound role culture and VoltDrive's fast-moving task culture — so a culture clash is likely, with significant consequences for VoltDrive's staff.

The most immediate consequence is demotivation. VoltDrive's engineers are used to autonomy, project teams and quick decisions; imposing AutoStark's bureaucracy, approval layers and hierarchy would strip away the autonomy that motivated them. Loss of autonomy removes a key motivator, so morale and effort are likely to fall.

A second consequence is loss of key talent. The most skilled engineers — precisely the people AutoStark paid to acquire — are also the most mobile, and rather than adapt to a slower, more restrictive structure they may leave for another start-up. This 'brain drain' would undermine the central rationale of the deal.

A third consequence is reduced innovation. Formal procedures and multiple sign-offs, characteristic of a role culture, would slow VoltDrive's rapid development cycle, frustrating staff and eroding the competitive edge the acquisition was meant to secure.

Against this, there are possible positives if the integration is handled well. VoltDrive employees may gain job security, better pay and benefits, and access to AutoStark's far larger R&D budget and manufacturing scale — resources a start-up could never fund alone. The bigger organization may also offer clearer career progression than a small firm could.

**Judgement.** On balance, the consequences for VoltDrive's employees are likely to be negative in the short to medium term, and the outcome depends decisively on how AutoStark manages the integration. The fundamental conflict between a fast task culture and a slow role culture is deep, so unless AutoStark deliberately protects VoltDrive's way of working — for example by running it as a semi-autonomous subsidiary, shielding its teams from bureaucracy and retaining its leaders — morale, talent and innovation are all at high risk. The financial and career benefits are real but will not retain innovative engineers who feel stifled. The decisive factor is therefore the post-acquisition integration strategy: handled sensitively, the deal can work; handled as a simple takeover, it is likely to destroy the very talent and dynamism it sought to buy.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks** — 'evaluate' needs AO1, AO2 and a balanced AO3 ending in a supported judgement:

- **AO1 — knowledge:** credits correct use of culture clash, role culture, task culture and the link between autonomy and motivation.
- **AO2 — application:** credits tying these to the two firms — VoltDrive's engineers, AutoStark's approval layers, the innovation cycle, the acquisition rationale.
- **AO3 — balanced evaluation + judgement:** credits weighing negatives (demotivation, talent loss, slower innovation) against positives (security, resources, progression), then committing to a judgement that pins the outcome on the integration strategy and justifies why.
- **Top band vs lower band:** an applied, two-sided answer ending in a reasoned decision reaches the top band; a one-sided or generic list of 'problems with mergers' does not, and a 'judgement' that only restates both sides cannot.

items: **Confusing power and task culture** — in a POWER culture authority sits with a central person regardless of expertise; in a TASK culture it shifts to whoever has the relevant expertise. Fast decisions alone do not make a culture a power culture — identify WHERE the power comes from.

**Confusing culture with structure** — structure is the formal chart of roles and reporting lines; culture is how people actually behave within it. Redrawing the chart does not change the culture, and treating the two as the same weakens the answer.

**Assuming culture can be changed quickly** — announcing new values, a rebrand or a fresh mission statement is not culture change. Real change means shifting ingrained habits and assumptions, which is slow, costly and resisted — say so explicitly.

**Classifying a culture with no evidence** — writing 'this is a role culture' with no case-study evidence earns AO1 at best; the AO2 application marks come from the evidence link (procedures, hierarchy, project teams, autonomy).
**Treating a merger's failure as purely financial** — culture clash is a leading cause of M&A failure; ignoring the human/cultural side misses the point of a 2.5 question.

**Listing types or challenges without applying them** — a bare list of Handy's four types or of 'why culture is hard to change' earns AO1 only; the marks climb when each point is tied to the specific business.
**Evaluating without a supported judgement** — an 'evaluate' or 'discuss' answer that gives both sides but never commits to a justified conclusion cannot reach the top band.

sections.content: Organizational culture connects to much of the rest of the course. It sits alongside leadership and management styles (2.4) and motivation (2.6), because the culture a leader builds shapes how those styles land; it feeds into organizational structure (2.2), since structure and culture must fit each other; and it returns forcefully in the growth-and-evolution unit, where mergers, acquisitions and multinational expansion succeed or fail on cultural integration. Master the habit built here — identify the culture concept, apply it to the specific business, weigh both sides, then commit to a justified judgement — and you have the template that earns marks across every evaluation question in Business Management.
